title: Official language

infobox.value: is defined by the [Cambridge English Dictionary](/wiki/Cambridge_English_Dictionary "Cambridge English Dictionary") as, "the [language](/wiki/Language "Language") or one of the languages that is accepted by a country's government, is taught in schools, used in the courts of law, etc."[[1]](#cite_note-1) Depending on the decree, establishment of an official language might also place restrictions on the use of other languages in those capacities.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Designated rights of an official language can be created in written form or by historic usage.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Language given special status in a country or territory

Not to be confused with [national language](/wiki/National_language "National language").

An **official language** is defined by the [Cambridge English Dictionary](/wiki/Cambridge_English_Dictionary "Cambridge English Dictionary") as, "the [language](/wiki/Language "Language") or one of the languages that is accepted by a country's government, is taught in schools, used in the courts of law, etc."[[1]](#cite_note-1) Depending on the decree, establishment of an official language might also place restrictions on the use of other languages in those capacities.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Designated rights of an official language can be created in written form or by historic usage.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

An official language is recognized by 178 countries, of which 101 recognize more than one.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The government of Italy made [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") their official language in 1999,[[6]](#cite_note-lang-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) and some nations (such as Mexico and Australia) have never declared *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* official languages at the national level.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Other nations have declared non-indigenous official languages.

Many of the world's constitutions mention one or more official or [national languages](/wiki/National_language "National language").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Some countries use the official language designation to empower indigenous groups by giving them access to the government in their native languages. In countries that do not formally designate an official language, a *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* national language usually evolves. [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is the most common official or co-official language, with recognized status in 52 countries. [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") are official or co-official languages in several countries.

An official language that is also an [indigenous language](/wiki/Indigenous_language "Indigenous language") is called *endoglossic*, one that is not indigenous is *exoglossic*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) An instance is Nigeria, which has three endoglossic [official languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Nigeria "Languages of Nigeria"). By this, the country aims to protect the indigenous languages although at the same time recognising the English language as its lingua franca. In spatial terms, [indigenous (endoglossic) languages](/wiki/Indigenous_language "Indigenous language") are mostly employed in the function of official languages in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), while mainly non-indigenous (exoglossic) rest of the world.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Around 500 BC, when [Darius the Great](/wiki/Darius_the_Great "Darius the Great") annexed [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") to the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), he chose a form of the [Aramaic language](/wiki/Aramaic_language "Aramaic language") (the so-called [Official Aramaic](/wiki/Official_Aramaic "Official Aramaic") or Imperial Aramaic) as the vehicle for written communication between the different regions of the vast empire with its different peoples and languages.[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Aramaic script](/wiki/Aramaic_script "Aramaic script") was widely employed from [Egypt](/wiki/History_of_Achaemenid_Egypt "History of Achaemenid Egypt") in the southwest to [Bactria](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") and [Sogdiana](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana") in the northeast. Texts were dictated in the native dialects and written down in Aramaic, and then read out again in the native language at the places they were received.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang"), the first [emperor of China](/wiki/Emperor_of_China "Emperor of China"), standardized the written language of China after unifying the country in 221 BC.[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese") would remain the standard written language for the next 2000 years. Standardization of the spoken language received less political attention, and [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") developed on an ad hoc basis from the dialects of the various imperial capitals until being officially standardized in the early twentieth century.

## Statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Statistics")]

See also: [List of official languages § List of languages by number of countries in which they are the official language](/wiki/List_of_official_languages#List_of_languages_by_number_of_countries_in_which_they_are_the_official_language "List of official languages")

The following languages are official (*de jure* or *de facto*) in two or more sovereign states. In some cases, a language may be defined as different languages in different countries. Examples are Hindi and Urdu, Malay and Indonesian, Serbian and Croatian, Persian and Tajik.

* English: [58 UN states and 31 dependencies](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_English_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where English is an official language")
* French: [26 UN states and 10 dependencies](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_French_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where French is an official language")
* Arabic: [23 UN states and Palestine, 2 non-UN states on the same territory, and 1 dependency](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Arabic_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Arabic is an official language")
* Spanish: [20 UN states and 1 dependency](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Spanish_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Spanish is an official language")
* Portuguese: [9 UN states and 1 dependency](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Portuguese_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Portuguese is an official language")
* German: [6 UN states](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_German_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where German is an official language")
* Russian: [5 UN and 3 unrecognized states](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Russian_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Russian is an official language")
* Serbo-Croatian: [4 UN states and 1 partially recognized state on the same territory](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian")
* Malay: [4 UN states and 1 dependency](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Malay_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Malay is an official language")
* Swahili: [4 UN states (5 counting Comorian)](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language")
* Italian: [3 UN states and Vatican City](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Italian_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Italian is an official language")
* Persian: [3 UN states and 1 dependency](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Persian_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Persian is an official language")
* Dutch: [3 UN states (4 counting Afrikaans)](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Afrikaans_or_Dutch_are_official_languages "List of countries and territories where Afrikaans or Dutch are official languages")
* Somali: [3 UN states and 1 unrecognized state on the same territory](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language")
* Sotho: [3 UN states](/wiki/Sotho_language "Sotho language")
* Standard Chinese (Mandarin): [2 UN states and Taiwan](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Chinese_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Chinese is an official language")
* Tamil: [2 UN states and 2 States in India](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Tamil_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Tamil is an official language")
* Hindustani: [2 UN states (counting Fijian Hindi) and 10 states in India](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Hindustani_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Hindustani is an official language")
* Bengali: [2 UN states (counting Bangladesh) and 1 state in India](/w/index.php?title=List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Bengali_is_an_official_language&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of countries and territories where Bengali is an official language (page does not exist)")
* Greek: [2 UN states](/wiki/Lists_of_countries_and_territories_by_official_language#Greek "Lists of countries and territories by official language")
* Turkish: [2 UN states and 1 partially recognized state](/wiki/Lists_of_countries_and_territories_by_official_language#Turkish "Lists of countries and territories by official language")

Some countries—like [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")—have no official language recognized as such at a national level. On the other extreme, [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") officially recognizes 37 languages, the most of any country in the world. Second to Bolivia is [India](/wiki/India "India") with [22 official languages](/wiki/Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India "Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India"). [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") has 16 official languages, [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") has 13, and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") is the country with the fifth lead with 12 official languages that all have equal status;[[16]](#cite_note-ZAConst-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Bolivia gives primacy to [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and India gives primacy to [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language") .[[18]](#cite_note-economist.com-18)

## Political alternatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political alternatives")]

See also: [List of multilingual countries and regions](/wiki/List_of_multilingual_countries_and_regions "List of multilingual countries and regions")

The selection of an official language (or the lack thereof) is often contentious.[[19]](#cite_note-Patten-19) An alternative to having a single official language is "official [multilingualism](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism")", where a government recognizes multiple official languages. Under this system, all government services are available in all official languages. Each [citizen](/wiki/Citizen "Citizen") may choose their preferred language when conducting business. Most countries are multilingual[[20]](#cite_note-20) and many are officially multilingual. [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") are examples of official multilingualism. This has been described as controversial and, in some other areas where it has been proposed, the idea has been rejected.[[19]](#cite_note-Patten-19) It has also been described as necessary for the recognition of different groups[[21]](#cite_note-21) or as an advantage for the country in presenting itself to outsiders.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Official languages by country and territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Official languages by country and territory")]

Main article: [List of official languages by country and territory](/wiki/List_of_official_languages_by_country_and_territory "List of official languages by country and territory")

### Afghanistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Afghanistan")]

Main article: [Languages of Afghanistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Afghanistan "Languages of Afghanistan")

According to the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban"), the Afghan government gives equal status to [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language") and [Dari](/wiki/Dari_language "Dari language") as official languages.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Azerbaijan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Azerbaijan")]

Main article: [Languages of Azerbaijan](/wiki/Languages_of_Azerbaijan "Languages of Azerbaijan")

Article 21 of [Azerbaijani Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Azerbaijan "Constitution of Azerbaijan") designates the official language of the [Republic of Azerbaijan](/wiki/Republic_of_Azerbaijan "Republic of Azerbaijan") as [Azerbaijani Language](/wiki/Azerbaijani_Language "Azerbaijani Language").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

After the independence of Bangladesh in 1971, [Sheikh Mujibur Rahman](/wiki/Sheikh_Mujibur_Rahman "Sheikh Mujibur Rahman"), the first [President of Bangladesh](/wiki/President_of_Bangladesh "President of Bangladesh") adopted the policy of 'one state one language'.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* [national language](/wiki/National_language "National language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), is the sole official language of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") according to the third article of the [Constitution of Bangladesh](/wiki/Constitution_of_Bangladesh "Constitution of Bangladesh").[[27]](#cite_note-bdlaws.minlaw.gov.bd-27) The government of Bangladesh introduced the [Bengali Language Implementation Act, 1987](/wiki/Bangla_Bhasha_Procholon_Ain,_1987 "Bangla Bhasha Procholon Ain, 1987") to ensure the mandatory use of Bengali in all government affairs.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Belarus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Belarus")]

Main article: [Belarusian since 1991](/wiki/Belarusian_language#History "Belarusian language")

[Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") have official status in the [Republic of Belarus](/wiki/Republic_of_Belarus "Republic of Belarus").

### Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Belgium")]

[Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") has three official languages: [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

[Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language") is the sole official language in [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Official bilingualism in Canada](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada")

Following the Constitution Act, 1982 the (federal) [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") gives equal status to English and French as official languages. The Province of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") is also officially bilingual, as is [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"). [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") has four official languages: English, French, [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut "Inuktitut") and [Inuinnaqtun](/wiki/Inuinnaqtun "Inuinnaqtun"). The [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") has eleven official languages: [Chipewyan/Dené](/wiki/Chipewyan_language "Chipewyan language"), [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language"), [English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English"), [French](/wiki/Canadian_French "Canadian French"), [Gwich’in](/wiki/Gwich%E2%80%99in_language "Gwich’in language"), [Inuinnaqtun](/wiki/Inuinnaqtun "Inuinnaqtun"), [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut "Inuktitut"), [Inuvialuktun](/wiki/Inuvialuktun "Inuvialuktun"), [North Slavey](/wiki/Slavey_language "Slavey language"), [South Slavey](/wiki/Slavey_language "Slavey language"), and [Tłı̨chǫ](/wiki/Dogrib_language "Dogrib language") (Dogrib). All provinces, however, offer some necessary services in both English and French.

The Province of Quebec with the [Official Language Act (Quebec)](/wiki/Official_Language_Act_(Quebec) "Official Language Act (Quebec)") and [Charter of the French Language](/wiki/Charter_of_the_French_Language "Charter of the French Language") defines French, the language of the majority of the population, as the official language of the provincial government.

### Ethiopia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ethiopia")]

Main article: [Languages of Ethiopia](/wiki/Languages_of_Ethiopia "Languages of Ethiopia")

Ethiopia has five official languages (Amharic alone until 2020) [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language"), [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_language "Oromo language"), [Somali](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language"), [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language"), and [Afar](/wiki/Afar_language "Afar language"), but Amharic is the de facto sole official language which is used by the government for issuing driving licenses, business licenses, passport, and foreign diplomacy with the addition that Court documents are in Amharic, and the constitution is written in Amharic, making Amharic a higher official language in the country.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Finland")]

According to the Finnish constitution, [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") are the *national languages* of the republic, giving their speakers the right to communicate with, and receive official documents from, government authorities in either of the two languages in any part of the country – making those languages de facto *official*.[[32]](#cite_note-FIN_constitution-32) Speakers of [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages") have those same rights in their native area (*Sámi homeland*).[[33]](#cite_note-FIN_Sámi_language_act-33)

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Germany")]

Main article: [Languages of Germany](/wiki/Languages_of_Germany "Languages of Germany")

Its [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") include [Sorbian](/wiki/Sorbian_language "Sorbian language") ([Upper Sorbian](/wiki/Upper_Sorbian "Upper Sorbian") and [Lower Sorbian](/wiki/Lower_Sorbian "Lower Sorbian")), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") and [North Frisian](/wiki/North_Frisian_language "North Frisian language"), which are officially recognised.

### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

Main article: [Languages of Hong Kong](/wiki/Languages_of_Hong_Kong "Languages of Hong Kong")

According to the [Basic Law of Hong Kong](/wiki/Basic_Law_of_Hong_Kong "Basic Law of Hong Kong") and the [Official Languages Ordinance](/wiki/Official_Languages_Ordinance "Official Languages Ordinance"), both [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") and [English](/wiki/Hong_Kong_English "Hong Kong English") are the official languages of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") with equal status. The variety of Chinese is not stipulated; however, [Cantonese](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cantonese "Hong Kong Cantonese"), being the language most commonly used by the majority of [Hongkongers](/wiki/Hongkongers "Hongkongers"), forms the *de facto* standard. Similarly, [Traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters") are most commonly used in Hong Kong and form the *de facto* standard for written Chinese, however, there is an increasing presence of [Simplified Chinese characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") particularly in areas related to tourism.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In government use, documents written using Traditional [Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese") characters are authoritative over ones written with Simplified Chinese characters.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: India")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Trilingual_Signboard_leading_to_Reservation_Office.jpg/250px-Trilingual_Signboard_leading_to_Reservation_Office.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trilingual_Signboard_leading_to_Reservation_Office.jpg)

Trilingual signboard in [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Hindi](/wiki/Standard_Hindi "Standard Hindi") in [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha") state of India

Further information: [Languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India") and [Languages with official recognition in India](/wiki/Languages_with_official_recognition_in_India "Languages with official recognition in India")

The [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") ([part 17](/wiki/Part_XVII_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Part XVII of the Constitution of India")) designates the official language of the Government of India as [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") written in the Devanagari script.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Although the original intentions of the constitution were to phase out English as an official language, provisions were provided so that "Parliament may by law provide for the use ... of ... the English language".

The [Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution](/wiki/Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India "Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India") lists has 22 languages,[[37]](#cite_note-37) which have been referred to as scheduled languages and given recognition, status and official encouragement. In addition, the Government of India has awarded the distinction of classical language to [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") and [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language").

### Indonesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Indonesia")]

Main article: [Indonesian language](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language")

The official language of Indonesia is the [Indonesian language](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") (*Bahasa Indonesia*). Bahasa Indonesia is regulated in Chapter XV, 1945 Constitution of Indonesia.

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Israel")]

Main article: [Languages of Israel](/wiki/Languages_of_Israel "Languages of Israel")

On 19 July 2018, the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") passed a [basic law](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Israel "Basic Laws of Israel") under the title [Israel as the Nation-State of the Jewish People](/wiki/Basic_Law:_Israel_as_the_Nation-State_of_the_Jewish_People "Basic Law: Israel as the Nation-State of the Jewish People"), which defines [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew") as "the State's language" and Arabic as a language with "a special status in the State" (article 4). The law further says that it should not be interpreted as compromising the status of the [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") language in practice before the enactment of the basic law, namely, it preserves the status quo and changes the status of Hebrew and Arabic only nominally.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Before the enactment of the aforementioned basic law, the status of official language in Israel was determined by the 82nd paragraph of the "[Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") [Order in Council](/wiki/Order_in_Council "Order in Council")" issued on [14 August 1922](https://web.archive.org/web/20081221030238/http://domino.un.org/UNISPAL.NSF/361eea1cc08301c485256cf600606959/c7aae196f41aa055052565f50054e656!OpenDocument), for the [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine"), as amended in 1939:[[39]](#cite_note-39)

:   "All Ordinances, official notices and official forms of the Government and all official notices of local authorities and municipalities in areas to be prescribed by order of the High Commissioner, shall be published in English, Arabic, and Hebrew."

This law, like most other laws of the British Mandate, was adopted in the State of Israel, subject to certain amendments published by the provisional legislative branch on 19 May 1948. The amendment states that:

:   "Any provision in the law requiring the use of the English language is repealed."[[40]](#cite_note-IL1-40)

In most [public schools](/wiki/State_school "State school"), the main teaching language is Hebrew, English is taught as a second language, and most students learn a third language, usually Arabic but not necessarily. Other public schools have Arabic as their main teaching language, and they teach Hebrew as a second language and English as a third one. There are also bilingual schools which aim to teach both Hebrew and Arabic equally.

Some languages other than Hebrew and Arabic, such as English, Russian, [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic"), [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") and [Ladino](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish") enjoy a somewhat special status but are not official languages. For instance, at least 5% of the broadcasting time of privately owned TV channels must be translated into Russian (a similar privilege is granted to Arabic), warnings must be translated to several languages, and signs are mostly trilingual (Hebrew, Arabic and English), and the government supports Yiddish and Ladino culture (alongside Hebrew culture and Arabic culture).

### Latvia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Latvia")]

Main article: [Languages of Latvia](/wiki/Languages_of_Latvia "Languages of Latvia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Street_sign._Lenin_street._Riga_%281991%29.png/250px-Street_sign._Lenin_street._Riga_%281991%29.png)](/wiki/File:Street_sign._Lenin_street._Riga_(1991).png)

A former [name sign](/wiki/Street_name_sign "Street name sign") on "Lenin Street" in the two official languages at the time of the 1945–1991 [Soviet](/wiki/Latvian_SSR "Latvian SSR") [occupation of](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states") [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"): [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language") (above) and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (below, in [Cyrillic alphabet](/wiki/Cyrillic_alphabet "Cyrillic alphabet"))

The Official Language Law recognizes [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language") as the sole official language of Latvia, while [Latgalian](/wiki/Latgalian_language "Latgalian language") is protected as "a historic variant of Latvian" and [Livonian](/wiki/Livonian_language "Livonian language") is recognized as "the language of the indigenous (autochthonous) population".[[41]](#cite_note-language_law-41) Latvia also provides national minority education programmes in [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew"), [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"), [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), and [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language").[[42]](#cite_note-42) In 2012 there was a [constitutional referendum](/wiki/2012_Latvian_constitutional_referendum "2012 Latvian constitutional referendum") on elevating [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") as a co-official language, but the proposal was rejected by nearly three-quarters of the voters.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

Main article: [Malay language](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language")

The official language of Malaysia is the [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") (*Bahasa Melayu*), also known as Bahasa Malaysia or just Bahasa for short. Bahasa Melayu is being protected under Article 152 of the Constitution of Malaysia.

### Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Netherlands")]

[Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") is the official language of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") (a constituent country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands). In the province of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), [Frisian](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language") is the official second language. While Dutch is therefore the official language of the [Caribbean Netherlands](/wiki/Caribbean_Netherlands "Caribbean Netherlands") (the islands [Bonaire](/wiki/Bonaire "Bonaire"), [Saba](/wiki/Saba_(island) "Saba (island)") and [Sint Eustatius](/wiki/Sint_Eustatius "Sint Eustatius")), it is not any of the three islands' main spoken language: [Papiamento](/wiki/Papiamento "Papiamento") is the most often spoken language on Bonaire, while [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is on both Saba and Sint Eustatius. These languages can be used in official documents (but do not have the same status as Frisian). [Low Saxon](/wiki/West_Low_German "West Low German") and [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish "Limburgish"), languages acknowledged by the [European Charter](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"), are spoken in specific regions of the Netherlands.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Main article: [Languages of New Zealand](/wiki/Languages_of_New_Zealand "Languages of New Zealand")

[New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") has three official languages. English is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* official language, accepted as such in all situations. The [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") and [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language") both have restricted *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* official status under the [Māori Language Act 2016](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Act_2016 "Māori Language Act 2016") and [New Zealand Sign Language Act 2006](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language_Act_2006 "New Zealand Sign Language Act 2006").[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 2018, [New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First") MP [Clayton Mitchell](/wiki/Clayton_Mitchell_(New_Zealand_politician) "Clayton Mitchell (New Zealand politician)") introduced a bill to parliament to [statutorily](/wiki/Statute "Statute") recognise English as an official language. As of May 2020, the bill had not progressed.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) During the [2023 New Zealand general election](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election"), New Zealand First leader [Winston Peters](/wiki/Winston_Peters "Winston Peters") campaigned to make English an official language, and in 2026 [a relevant bill](/wiki/English_Language_Bill "English Language Bill") received its first reading.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Nigeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Nigeria")]

Main article: [Languages of Nigeria](/wiki/Languages_of_Nigeria "Languages of Nigeria")

The official language of Nigeria is English, which was chosen to facilitate the cultural and linguistic unity of the country. [British colonial rule](/wiki/Colonial_Nigeria "Colonial Nigeria") ended in 1960.

### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Norway")]

Main articles: [Languages of Norway](/wiki/Languages_of_Norway "Languages of Norway") and [Norwegian language conflict](/wiki/Norwegian_language_conflict "Norwegian language conflict")

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Main article: [Languages of Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan")

[Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and English both are official languages in Pakistan. Pakistan has more than 60 other languages. Among these, [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language") exists as an official language in the province of [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh").

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main article: [Languages of the Philippines](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Philippines "Languages of the Philippines")

[Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_language "Filipino language") and English both are official languages of the Philippines.

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Poland")]

Main article: [Languages of Poland](/wiki/Languages_of_Poland "Languages of Poland")

[Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") is the official language of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland").

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main article: [Languages of Russia](/wiki/Languages_of_Russia "Languages of Russia")

[Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") is the official language of the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russian_Federation "Russian Federation") and in all [federal subjects](/wiki/Federal_subjects_of_Russia "Federal subjects of Russia"), however many minority languages have official status in the areas where they are indigenous. One type of federal subject in Russia,
[republics](/wiki/Republics_of_Russia "Republics of Russia"), are allowed to adopt additional official languages alongside Russian in their constitutions. Republics are often based around particular native ethnic groups and are often areas where ethnic [Russians](/wiki/Russian_people "Russian people") and native Russian-language speakers are a minority.

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: South Africa")]

Main article: [Languages of South Africa](/wiki/Languages_of_South_Africa "Languages of South Africa")

[South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") has twelve official languages[[16]](#cite_note-ZAConst-16) that are mostly indigenous. Due to limited funding, however, the government rarely produces documents in most languages. Accusations of mismanagement and [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_South_Africa "Corruption in South Africa") have been leveled[[52]](#cite_note-Sowetan-52) against the [Pan South African Language Board](/wiki/Pan_South_African_Language_Board "Pan South African Language Board"), established to promote multilingualism, develop the 11 official languages, and protect language rights in the country.[[18]](#cite_note-economist.com-18) In practice, government is conducted in English.

### Switzerland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Switzerland")]

Main article: [Languages of Switzerland](/wiki/Languages_of_Switzerland "Languages of Switzerland")

The four national languages of [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") are [German](/wiki/Swiss_Standard_German "Swiss Standard German"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language"). At the federal level German, French and Italian are official languages, the official languages of individual cantons depend on the languages spoken in them.

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

[Mandarin](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin") is the most common language used in government. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the mainland Chinese-run government made Mandarin the official language, and it was used in the schools and government. Under the Development of National Languages Act, political participation can be conducted in any national language, which is defined as a "natural language used by an original people group of Taiwan",[[53]](#cite_note-53) which also includes [Formosan languages](/wiki/Formosan_languages "Formosan languages"), the [Taiwanese variety of Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien") and [Hakka](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hakka "Taiwanese Hakka"). According to Taiwan's Legislative Yuan, amendments were made to the Hakka Basic Act to make [Hakka](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hakka "Taiwanese Hakka") an official language of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Timor-Leste

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Timor-Leste")]

According to the [constitution of Timor-Leste](/wiki/Constitution_of_Timor-Leste "Constitution of Timor-Leste"), [Tetum](/wiki/Tetum_language "Tetum language") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") are the official languages of the country, and every official document must be published in both languages; [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") hold "working language" status in the country.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

See also: [Languages of Ukraine](/wiki/Languages_of_Ukraine "Languages of Ukraine"), [Russification of Ukraine](/wiki/Russification_of_Ukraine "Russification of Ukraine"), [Language policy in Ukraine](/wiki/Language_policy_in_Ukraine "Language policy in Ukraine"), and [Law of Ukraine "On protecting the functioning of the Ukrainian language as the state language"](/wiki/Law_of_Ukraine_%22On_protecting_the_functioning_of_the_Ukrainian_language_as_the_state_language%22 "Law of Ukraine \"On protecting the functioning of the Ukrainian language as the state language\"")

The official language of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") is [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language").

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

See also: [Languages of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_Kingdom "Languages of the United Kingdom")

The de facto official language of the United Kingdom is [English](/wiki/English_language "English language").[[56]](#cite_note-56) In [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), the [Welsh language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), spoken by approximately 20% of the population, has *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* official status, alongside English.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) as of November 30, 2025 Gaelic and Scots have become official languages of Scotland alongside the earlier declared official language of English. [[2]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c9891455007o)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: United States")]

[![Map of United States Official Language Status By State](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/USA_states_english_official_language.svg/250px-USA_states_english_official_language.svg.png)](/wiki/File:USA_states_english_official_language.svg)

Map of US official language status by state before 2016. Blue: English as the official language; light-blue: English as a co-official language; gray: no official language specified.

See also: [Languages of the United States](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_States "Languages of the United States")

[English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is the predominant language of [the United States](/wiki/The_United_States "The United States"). While the U.S. has no *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* official language as no legislation has been passed to recognize English as the nation's official language, [Executive Order 14224](/wiki/Executive_Order_14224 "Executive Order 14224") of 2025 declares English official.[[59]](#cite_note-EOWP-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) Most states have passed legislation to designate English as their official language; 32 of the 50 [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._states "U.S. states")[[62]](#cite_note-62) and all five inhabited [U.S. territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States") have designated English as one, or the only, official language, while courts have found that residents in the 50 states do not have a right to government services in their preferred language.[[63]](#cite_note-Scholastic-63) Public debate in the last few decades has focused on whether [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") should be recognized by the government, or whether all business should be done in English.[[19]](#cite_note-Patten-19)

[California](/wiki/California "California") allows people to take their [driving test](/wiki/Driver%27s_license_in_the_United_States "Driver's license in the United States") in the following 32 languages: Amharic, Arabic, Armenian, Chinese, Croatian, English, French, German, Greek, Hebrew, Hindi, Hmong, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), Khmer, Korean, Laotian, [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), Polish, Portuguese, Punjabi, Romanian, Russian, Samoan, Spanish, Tagalog/Filipino, Thai, Tongan, Turkish, and Vietnamese.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

[New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") state provides voter-registration forms in the following five languages: [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"). The same languages are also on ballot papers in certain parts of the state (namely, [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")).[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Opponents of an official English language policy in the United States argue that it would hamper "the government's ability to reach out, communicate, and warn people in the event of a natural or man-made disaster such as a hurricane, pandemic, or...another terrorist attack".[[63]](#cite_note-Scholastic-63) Professor of politics [Alan Patten](/wiki/Alan_Patten "Alan Patten") argues that disengagement (officially ignoring the issue) works well in religious issues but that it is not possible with language issues because it must offer public services in some language.[[19]](#cite_note-Patten-19)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Yugoslavia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Yugoslavia")]

Sometimes an official language definition can be motivated more by national identity than by linguistic concerns. Prior to the [breakup in early 1990s](/wiki/Breakup_of_Yugoslavia "Breakup of Yugoslavia"), although [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") had no official language on the federal level, its six constituent republics including two autonomous provinces accounted for four official languages—[Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language") and [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"). Serbo-Croatian served as the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* for mutual understanding and was also the language of [the military](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army"), as official in four republics and taught as a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") in the other two.

When [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") declared independence in 1991, it defined its official language as [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), while the confederate union of [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro") likewise defined its official language as [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") in 1992. [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") defined three official languages: [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), Croatian, and Serbian. From the linguistic point of view, the different names refer to national varieties of the same language, which is known under the appellation of Serbo-Croatian.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)
The language used in [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") became standardized as the [Montenegrin language](/wiki/Montenegrin_language "Montenegrin language") upon Montenegro's declaration of independence from [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro") in 2006.

### Zimbabwe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Official_language&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Zimbabwe")]

Since the adoption of the [2013 Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Zimbabwe "Constitution of Zimbabwe"), Zimbabwe has 16 official languages, namely[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

* [Chewa](/wiki/Chewa_language "Chewa language")
* [Chibarwe](/wiki/Sena_language "Sena language")
* [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")
* [Kalanga](/wiki/Kalanga_language "Kalanga language")
* [Koisan](/wiki/Khoisan_languages "Khoisan languages")
* [Nambya](/wiki/Nambya_language "Nambya language")
* [Ndau](/wiki/Ndau_dialect "Ndau dialect")
* [Ndebele](/wiki/Northern_Ndebele_language "Northern Ndebele language")
* [Shangani](/wiki/Tsonga_language "Tsonga language")
* [Shona](/wiki/Shona_language "Shona language")
* [sign language](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language")
* [Sotho](/wiki/Sesotho_language "Sesotho language")
* [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga_language_(Zambia_and_Zimbabwe) "Tonga language (Zambia and Zimbabwe)")
* [Tswana](/wiki/Tswana_language "Tswana language")
* [Venda](/wiki/Venda_language "Venda language")
* [Xhosa](/wiki/Xhosa_language "Xhosa language")
